name: DSA
description: Data structures and algorithms: complexity, core structures, sorting, trees, graphs, and DP patterns.

units.title: Complexity and big-O
units.summary: Modeling cost, growth rates, and empirical measurement.

content.value: Big-O describes upper bounds on growth; Omega lower bounds; Theta tight bounds. Constants and lower-order terms drop as n grows—focus on dominant terms.
Amortized analysis averages cost over a sequence (e.g., dynamic array appends). Worst-case vs average-case differ—know your distribution.

content.code:
# Binary search O(log n) on sorted array
def bin_search(a, x):
    lo, hi = 0, len(a)-1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo+hi)//2
        if a[mid] == x: return mid
        elif a[mid] < x: lo = mid+1
        else: hi = mid-1
    return -1

content.value: Divide-and-conquer recurrences often look like T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n). The master method gives asymptotics for many common cases.
Substitution and recursion trees build intuition when the master theorem doesn’t apply cleanly.

theory.question: Define amortized time with an example.
theory.answer: Amortized time averages the total cost of a sequence of operations. A dynamic array doubling its capacity when full charges O(1) amortized per append even though some appends trigger O(n) resizing.

coding.title: Two-sum sorted
coding.description: Given sorted array, find two indices whose values sum to target in O(n) time.

content.value: Arrays offer O(1) index access and cache-friendly layout; inserts in the middle cost O(n). Linked lists offer O(1) inserts given a pointer but poor locality and no O(1) random access.
Choose structures based on access patterns: frequent tail operations favor deques; index-heavy workloads favor arrays.

content.code:
from collections import deque
q = deque()
q.append(1); q.appendleft(0); q.pop(); q.popleft()

content.value: Stacks are LIFO; queues FIFO. Both appear in graph algorithms, parsing, and scheduling.
Monotonic stacks solve next-greater-element style problems in linear time.

mcqs.question: Middle insertion is asymptotically cheaper in which structure (typical singly linked vs array)?
options: Linked list if you have a node pointer

theory.question: Why are arrays more cache-friendly than linked lists?
theory.answer: Array elements are contiguous in memory, improving spatial locality and CPU prefetching. Linked list nodes scatter across the heap, causing more cache misses when traversing.

coding.title: Reverse linked list
coding.description: Implement iterative reversal of a singly linked list in O(n) time and O(1) extra space.

units.title: Hashing and sorting
units.summary: Hash tables, collision strategies, classic sorts.

content.value: Hash maps map keys to slots via a hash function; collisions handled by chaining or open addressing. Expected O(1) ops if load factor is bounded and hashing is uniform.
Universal hashing reduces adversarial collisions; cryptographic hashes are overkill for containers.

content.code: # Python dict is a highly optimized hash table

content.value: Comparison sorts lower bound is Omega(n log n). QuickSort average O(n log n), worst O(n^2) without pivots; MergeSort stable O(n log n) worst case; HeapSort in-place O(n log n).
Non-comparison sorts (counting, radix) beat the log factor when keys are constrained.

content.code:
def counting_sort(a, k):
    c=[0]*(k+1)
    for x in a: c[x]+=1
    return [i for i,v in enumerate(c) for _ in range(v)]

mcqs.question: Average-case find in a good hash table is typically:
Which sort is stable by default in typical implementations?

theory.question: Why can counting sort beat O(n log n)?
theory.answer: It avoids comparisons by exploiting bounded integer keys—using direct indexing into counts—so it is not a comparison sort and not subject to the n log n comparison lower bound.

coding.title: Top-K frequent
coding.description: Given integer array, return k most frequent elements.
coding.hint: Hash counts + heap or bucket by frequency.

content.value: Traversals: preorder (node-left-right), inorder on BST yields sorted order, postorder useful for deletion bottom-up.
Balanced BSTs (AVL, red-black) guarantee O(log n) height—critical for worst-case maps/sets.
Binary heaps are complete trees stored in arrays; support O(log n) insert/extract-min. Build-heap is O(n).

content.value: Heaps power Dijkstra’s algorithm, merging k sorted lists, and scheduling.

mcqs.question: In-order traversal of a valid BST visits values in:

theory.question: Why prefer balanced BSTs over naive BST?
theory.answer: A naive BST can degenerate to a linked list with height O(n) if insertions are sorted; balanced trees keep height O(log n), restoring expected logarithmic operations.

coding.title: Validate BST
coding.description: Given root, return whether the tree satisfies BST property.

content.value: Graphs encode relationships: adjacency lists are space-efficient for sparse graphs; matrices help dense graphs and fast edge queries.

content.code:
from collections import defaultdict
G = defaultdict(list)
G[0].append(1); G[1].append(2)

content.value: BFS explores layer by layer—shortest unweighted paths; DFS explores deep paths first—good for connectivity and topological sort on DAGs.
Dijkstra handles non-negative edge weights with a priority queue. Bellman-Ford tolerates negative edges (no negative cycles). Floyd-Warshall all-pairs O(n^3).

content.value: For unweighted graphs, BFS already yields shortest path lengths.

mcqs.question: BFS on an unweighted graph from s finds:

theory.question: When does Dijkstra fail?
theory.answer: With negative edge weights (even without negative cycles), the greedy relaxation order breaks; use Bellman-Ford or specialized variants instead.

coding.title: Connected components
coding.description: Count connected components in an undirected graph given n nodes and edge list.
coding.hint: Union-find or DFS from each unseen node.

content.value: DP applies when optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems hold. Top-down memoization is recursive with cache; bottom-up tabulation fills tables iteratively.

content.code:
def fib(n, memo={}):
    if n <= 1: return n
    if n in memo: return memo[n]
    memo[n] = fib(n-1, memo) + fib(n-2, memo)
    return memo[n]

content.value: State definition is the hard part—encode minimal information to make transitions well-defined.

content.code: # 0-1 knapsack: dp[i][w] using first i items with weight w

content.value: LCS, edit distance, coin change, and subset sums illustrate 1D/2D recurrences. Often compress dimensions when only previous row matters.
Practice translating recurrences to iterative code to avoid stack limits.

content.code: # DP on trees: subtree states

theory.question: Give an example where greedy fails but DP works.
theory.answer: 0-1 knapsack with arbitrary values—greedy by value/weight ratio fails, while DP over items and capacity finds the optimum.

coding.title: Climbing stairs variants
coding.description: Count ways to reach step n if you can take 1 or 2 steps at a time—extend to 1,2,3 steps.
coding.hint: Linear recurrence; matrix exponentiation for huge n.
